book_claims.claim: Yogurt (particularly goat's milk yogurt) is used as a snack in the Longevity Diet at 125 g per serving.
book_claims.mechanism: Fermented dairy provides probiotics that support gut microbiome diversity, a key longevity factor. Goat's milk has a different protein and fat profile from cow's milk and has historically been part of the diet of long-lived populations in Sardinia and southern Italy.

book_claims.claim: Goat's milk and yogurt were consumed at approximately 84 grams per day in the Biosphere 2 longevity experiment diet.
book_claims.mechanism: Small amounts of fermented dairy provide protein, calcium, and probiotics without the larger IGF-1 and saturated fat burden of heavy dairy consumption.

book_claims.claim: Goat's milk yogurt is recommended for those over 65 who are losing muscle mass, as it is consumed in high-longevity areas.
book_claims.mechanism: Yogurt provides easily digestible protein and calcium; goat's milk proteins are smaller and more digestible than cow's milk proteins for many individuals, particularly useful for elderly populations at risk of sarcopenia.

book_claims.claim: Plain low-fat yogurt provides vitamin A (32 mcg RAE per cup, 116 IU, 2% DV).
book_claims.mechanism: Dairy retinol supports immune function, epithelial integrity, and vision; one of the micronutrients Longo tracks across the Longevity Diet.

supplementary_research.finding: PMID 27184265: Higher yogurt consumption was associated with lower all-cause mortality in a meta-analysis of 17 prospective cohorts; the association was particularly strong for cardiovascular mortality, consistent with the Longevity Diet's emphasis on fermented dairy.

PMID 26811459: Yogurt consumption is associated with reduced type 2 diabetes risk; the fermentation process and specific probiotic strains (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) improve insulin sensitivity, supporting metabolic longevity.

PMID 31484794: Probiotic-rich yogurt consumption improved gut microbiota diversity and reduced inflammatory markers (IL-6, CRP) in elderly subjects — a population specifically targeted by Longo's recommendation to use yogurt after age 65.

Examine confirms that the probiotics in yogurt (L. bulgaricus, S. thermophilus) survive gastric transit and colonize the gut transiently, improving lactose tolerance and immune function; Greek yogurt provides ~2× the protein of regular yogurt.

culinary_pairings.source: Longevity breakfast bowl; adds vitamin E and spermidine
Anthocyanin-rich berries pair with probiotic yogurt for gut health
Prebiotic + probiotic synergy; traditional Cretan breakfast

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: ~10 g (Greek) / ~3.5 g (regular plain low fat) per 100 g
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: High DIAAS; casein and whey proteins from yogurt are slowly and rapidly digested respectively, providing sustained amino acid availability.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: ~183 mg per 100 g plain low fat (~18% DV)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Lactic acid from fermentation increases calcium bioavailability compared to unfermented milk; important for bone health in aging populations.
Surviving probiotic bacteria transiently colonize the colon, producing short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate) that feed colonocytes and modulate immune function.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: ~32 mcg RAE per 240 mL (1 cup) plain low fat
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Retinol in dairy is highly bioavailable; supports epithelial cell integrity and immune surveillance.

synergies.description: Wheat germ adds spermidine (autophagy inducer), vitamin E, and folate to the probiotic and protein base of yogurt; a synergistic longevity breakfast pairing.
Walnuts add omega-3 ALA, polyphenols, and prebiotic fiber to yogurt; the combination supports gut microbiome diversity from both the probiotic (yogurt) and prebiotic (walnuts) sides.
Both goat/sheep milk fermented dairy products recommended in the Longevity Diet; together they provide a Mediterranean dairy pattern associated with longevity in Sardinia and Crete.
